1: ʾēl-nĕqāmôt YHWH ʾēl nĕqāmôt hôphîaʿ — O LORD, God of vengeance — God of justice, arise and shine forth! Nĕqāmôt (plural of nĕqāmāh: retribution, vengeance — from nāqam: to avenge, execute retributive justice). The plural may intensify (great retribution) or refer to multiple acts of just retribution. The term is not personal revenge but the legal execution of justice — YHWH as the impartial judge who balances the scales. Hôphîaʿ (hiphil imperative of yāphaʿ: to shine forth, appear in splendor — the request for the divine judicial appearance to become visible).
2: hinnāśēʾ shōpheṭ hāʾāreṣ — rise up, Judge of the earth. Nāśāʾ (niphal imperative: rise up — the judge taking his seat of authority). Shōphēṭ hāʾāreṣ (Judge of the earth) — the title Abraham used in his intercession for Sodom (Gen 18:25: hashōphēṭ kol-hāʾāreṣ lōʾ yaʿăśeh mishpāṭ). hāshēb gĕmûl ʿal-gēʾîm — pay back to the proud what they deserve. Gĕmûl (recompense, what is owed — the return that matches the deed) on the gēʾîm (proud, arrogant ones — from gāʾāh: to swell, rise high).
3: ʿad-māthay rĕshāʿîm YHWH ʿad-māthay rĕshāʿîm yaʿălōzû — how long, LORD, will the wicked — how long will the wicked rejoice in triumph? The doubled ʿad-māthay (how long? how long?) — the urgency of the theodicy question expressed through repetition. ʿĀlaz (to exult, rejoice triumphantly — often the celebration of an unexpected victory). The wicked's exulting is the affront: they should be silent before the divine judge, but instead they celebrate.
4: yabbîʿû yĕdabbĕrû ʿātāq — they spew proud words without end. Nābaʿ (hiphil: to pour out, gush forth — the uncontrolled outpouring of speech). ʿātāq (proud, arrogant things — words of presumptuous arrogance, the speech of those who think they are beyond accountability). yitʾammĕrû kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen — all who do evil brag without restraint. ʾĀmar (hitpael: to show oneself great, boast) — the evil-doers' self-display as the antithesis of fearing God.
5: ʿammĕkhā YHWH yĕdakĕʾû — they crush your people, LORD. Dākhāʾ (piel: to crush, pound — the same root as Ps 90:3's dakkāʾ: crushed dust). The victims identified: ʿammĕkhā (your people) — which means the oppression is not only against the weak but against YHWH himself. wĕnaḥălātĕkhā yĕʿannû — and oppress those who belong to you. Naḥălāh (inheritance, property — Israel as YHWH's own possession). To oppress God's naḥălāh is to violate divine property — the crime is against the owner of the people, not only against the people.
6: ʾalmānāh wĕgēr yahărōgû — they murder the widow and the foreigner. ʾAlmānāh (widow) + gēr (resident alien, foreigner — the protected classes of Israelite law: Exod 22:21-24; Deut 24:17-22; Lev 19:9-10). These are precisely the ones the Mosaic law most explicitly protects. wîtômîm yĕrāṣĕḥû — and kill the fatherless. Yātôm (orphan) — the third member of the classic triad (widow, orphan, stranger) that appears throughout the prophets as the test of social justice (Isa 1:17; Jer 7:6; Zech 7:10; Mal 3:5).
7: wayyōʾmĕrû lōʾ yirʾeh-YAH — and they say, 'The LORD never sees it'. Yirʾeh-YAH (the LORD does not see) — the theological premise of the oppressor: practical atheism, the denial of divine observation. wĕlōʾ-yābîn ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb — the God of Jacob pays no attention. Bîn (to understand, perceive — judicial discernment). The ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb (God of Jacob) — the covenant God of the patriarchs, who should above all perceive the suffering of his people, is claimed by the oppressor not to notice. The psalmist's counter-argument follows in vv9-11.
8: bînû bōʿărîm bāʿām — grasp this, you most senseless among the people. Bîn (imperative: understand, discern) directed at the bōʿărîm (the brutish/senseless ones — cf. Ps 92:6: ʾîsh-baʿar). ûkhĕsîlîm māthay taskîlû — fools, when will you become wise? Kĕsîlîm (fools — the morally obtuse). The rhetorical address directly to the oppressors, inviting them to use the faculty of reason they are demonstrating they lack.
9: hănōṭaʿ ʾōzen hălōʾ yishmaʿ — the one who made the ear — does he not hear? The qal wahomer (from minor to major) argument: the ear's Creator must be able to do what the ear can do. Nāṭaʿ (to plant, to form — the delicate craftsmanship of forming the ear). If a human craftsman understands the instrument he has crafted, how much more the divine craftsman who created hearing itself. ʾim-yōṣēr ʿayin hălōʾ yabbîṭ — the one who formed the eye — does he not see? The parallel: the eye's Formation (yāṣar: the potter's crafting of the eye's form) implies the ability to see. The argument from creation to Creator's capacities.
10: hăyōsēr gôyim hălōʾ yôkhîaḥ — the one who corrects the nations — will he not call them to account? Yāsar (to discipline, correct, instruct — the instructive discipline that brings nations to order). If YHWH disciplines entire nations, how could he fail to call individual oppressors to account? hămĕlammēd ʾādām daʿat — the one who teaches all people. Limmēd (piel: to teach) — the divine pedagogy directed toward all humanity (ʾādām: the collective human being).
11: YHWH yōdēaʿ maḥshĕbôt ʾādām kî-hēmmāh hebel — the LORD knows human beings' thoughts — that they are nothing but vapor. The divine knowledge maḥshĕbôt ʾādām (the thoughts/plans of humanity) — YHWH knows them precisely and has evaluated them: hēmmāh hebel (they are vapor, emptiness — the Qoheleth word for the insubstantial transience of all human projects). This verse is quoted by Paul in 1 Cor 3:20 (kurios ginōskei tous dialogismous tōn sophōn: the Lord knows the reasonings of the wise, that they are futile) — applied to the futility of human wisdom compared to divine wisdom. The oppressors' plans are known to God and evaluated as hebel.
12: ʾashrê haggeber ʾăsher-tĕyassĕrennû YAH — blessed is the man whom you discipline, LORD. ʾAshrê (blessed — the beatitude formula; cf. Ps 1:1) + yāsar (to discipline, instruct): blessing comes through discipline. ûmitôrātekhā tĕlammĕdennû — and instruct from your teaching. Tôrāh (teaching, instruction — the Torah as the divine curriculum through which YHWH instructs his people). The disciplined person is blessed not despite the hardship but through it — the instruction given through suffering.
13: lĕhashqîṭ lô mîmê rāʿ — giving him rest in days of trouble. Hashqîṭ (hiphil of shāqaṭ: to give rest, to make quiet — the resting-from-trouble that the disciplined person receives). ʿad yikkāreh lārāshāʿ shāḥat — until a pit is dug for the wicked. The temporary nature of the trouble: the disciplined righteous person rests while the wicked's pit is being prepared. Shāḥat (pit — both literal and metaphorical: the grave/destruction that awaits).
14: kî lōʾ-yiṭōsh YHWH ʿammô — the LORD will not abandon his people. Nāṭash (to abandon, forsake, cast off — the action of leaving without looking back). wĕnaḥălātô lōʾ yaʿăzōb — or desert his inheritance. ʿĀzab (to abandon, leave) — the two verbs together (nāṭash + ʿāzab) reinforce the covenantal commitment: YHWH will not leave his naḥălāh (cf. Deut 4:31; 31:6; Josh 1:5). The confidence grounding the lament of vv3-11 and the petition of v1-2.
15: kî-ʿad-ṣedeq yāshûb mishpāṭ — for justice will return to the righteous. Shûb (to return — justice reverting to its proper place with the righteous after its apparent absence). wĕʾaḥărāyw kol-yishĕrê-lēb — and all who are upright in heart will pursue it. Yāshār-lēb (upright of heart — the opposite of the hardened heart of v8; the person whose inner compass is aligned correctly). The return of justice creates a rally of the upright — they pursue what has returned.
16: mî-yāqûm lî ʿim-mĕrēʿîm — who will stand up for me against the wicked? The personal lament-question: who will rise as an advocate against the mĕrēʿîm (those who do evil — piel participle of rāʿaʿ: to do harm)? mî-yityaṣṣēb lî ʿim-pōʿălê ʾāwen — who will take my side against evildoers? Yāṣab (hitpael: to station oneself, take a position as an advocate or champion). The implied answer: only YHWH, as vv17-19 confirm.
17: lûlê YHWH ʿezrātāh lî — if the LORD had not been my help. Lûlê (if not — the conditional of disaster: what would have happened without divine intervention). kimaʿaṭ shākhĕnāh dûmāh nafshî — I would very soon have lain in the silence of death. Dûmāh (silence — the silence of death; cf. Ps 115:17: lōʾ hammētîm yĕhallĕlû-YAH wĕlōʾ kol-yōrĕdê dûmāh: not the dead praise the LORD, not those going down to silence). The narrow escape: kimaʿaṭ (almost, nearly) — the Psalmist was almost in the silence of death but for YHWH's intervention.
18: ʾim-ʾāmartî māṭāh raglî — when I felt my foot giving way. Māṭāh (to be inclined, to slip — the foot losing its footing). The moment of crisis described with simplicity: the foot slipping is enough to indicate the totality of the threat. ḥasdĕkhā YHWH yisʿādēnî — your steadfast love, LORD, held me steady. Sāʿad (to support, uphold, prop up — the prop placed under the slipping foot). The divine ḥesed as the stabilizing force under the failing foot — the covenant love as the practical ground underfoot.
19: bĕrōb śarʿappay bĕqirbî — when anxious thoughts multiply inside me. Śarʿappîm (anxious thoughts, disquieting thoughts — from śāraph: to divide, split; the inner splitting of anxiety; hapax in this form). The internal experience: the multiplication of worrying thoughts within (bĕqirbî: inside me). tanhûmekhā yĕshaʿăshĕʿû nafshî — your comfort brings delight to my soul. Tanḥûm (consolation, comfort — from nāḥam: to comfort, console). Shaʿashuaʿ (to delight, to bring exquisite pleasure — a rare intensive form: the divine comfort not merely quieting the anxiety but producing delight in the soul).
20: hăyĕḥābrĕkhā kissēʾ hawwôt — can a corrupt throne have any fellowship with you? Ḥābar (to be associated with, have fellowship/partnership with) — can the throne of hawwôt (ruin, destruction, malicious plans — the corrupt power that enacts harm) have any association with YHWH? The implied answer is no — YHWH cannot be in covenant with or complicit in corrupt governance. yōṣēr ʿāmāl ʿălê-ḥōq — one that enacts harm through its own laws. ʿĀmāl (trouble, injustice) enacted ʿălê-ḥōq (by/through statute, through official legislation) — the oppression that uses legal forms to perpetrate injustice is what the psalm specifically addresses: structural evil embedded in law.
21: yāgôddû ʿal-nefesh ṣaddîq — they join forces against the lives of the righteous. Gādad (hitpolel: to band together, mass against — the organized assault of a group). wĕdām nāqî yarshîʾû — and condemn the innocent to death. Dām nāqî (innocent blood — the blood of the guiltless) condemned (rāshāʿ: piel: to declare guilty, to condemn as guilty — the judicial condemnation of the blameless). The corruption of the legal system reaches its apex: innocent blood condemned through legal process.
22: wayhî YHWH lî lĕmiśgāb — but the LORD is my strong fortress. Miśgāb (high fortress, elevated refuge — from śāgab: to be high, inaccessible; cf. Ps 91:14 where the trusting person is placed beyond reach). The turn from complaint to confidence: wĕʾĕlōhay lĕṣûr maḥsî — and my God is the rock where I take shelter. Ṣûr maḥsî (rock of my refuge) — the two bedrock images (fortress + rock) combined as the divine basis of security against the corrupt power of v20-21.
23: wayyāsheb ʿălêhem ʾet-ʾôwnām — he will pay them back for their sin. Shûb ʿāl (hiphil: to bring back upon — the divine recoil of their ʾāwen [iniquity, evil] back onto those who committed it). ûbĕrāʿātām yaṣmîtēm — and destroy them for their evil. Ṣāmat (hiphil: to cut off, silence, destroy completely — used of the wicked's destruction, cf. Ps 18:40; 88:16). yaṣmîtēm YHWH ʾĕlōhênû — the LORD our God will wipe them out. The closing reiteration of the divine name: the God who was invoked in v1 as the God of retributions delivers the retribution at the psalm's close.
